schools.summary: Augustine: after promising the Spirit's strength, he forewarns 'that you should not be offended'; Chrysostom recalls the synagogue-ban already agreed (9:22).
'Offended' = made to stumble/apostatize (Mt 5:29); refers to the world's hatred and the Spirit's witness just spoken (15:18-27).
Henry: Christ deals faithfully, telling the worst that they may count the cost; Calvin: forewarning arms them beforehand.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is from another passage and does not bear on v.1.

schools.summary: Made 'outcasts from the synagogues' (9:22; 12:42); the killer thinks he 'offers a sacrificial service' — murder mistaken for worship.
Calvin: a heavy trial to be banished as wicked from the assembly of the godly, facing reproach as well as death (1 Cor 4:12-13).
Clarke: excommunication stripped goods and fellowship (Ezra 10:8); Wesley thanks God that age of religious killing is largely past.
The cause of the hatred is ignorance of the Father and the Son (15:21; 1 Cor 2:8); men exclude the saints thinking they serve God.
Calvin: knowing the one cause — they know not God — lets the apostles boldly despise the persecutors' blind fury, without extenuating their guilt.
Clarke/Wesley: ignorance of God's benevolence and Christ's love is the fountain and root of all religious persecution.

themes: ignorance of the Father and Son

schools.summary: Forewarned, they will remember and find in the fulfilment proof of his omniscience and evidence of his presence (13:19; 14:29).
Chrysostom/Clarke: he withheld it 'at the beginning' lest they be discouraged while he was present to bear the shock himself (Wesley).
Calvin: not idle philosophy but doctrine for use — they are to lay up his words against the hour of trial.
Chrysostom: overcome by sorrow, the disciples are gently blamed and corrected for not asking where he goes.
Peter and Thomas had asked earlier (13:36; 14:5); now grief turns their thoughts on themselves and they cease asking.
Calvin: a consolation behind the reproof — knowing Christ is in heaven as their guardian fits them for the coming battles.

themes: I go to him that sent me

schools.summary: 'Sorrow hath filled' (perfect) — a grief so absorbing it leaves no room for thought of Christ's gain in returning to the Father.
'It is expedient for you' (cf. 11:50) — by departing Christ atones, ascends, and sends the Spirit to be present with all believers everywhere at once.
Calvin: he weans them by oath from the carnal desire to draw Christ down from heaven; his absence is truly advantageous.
Clarke: by God's order the Spirit cannot come unless Christ first die; Wesley: expedient in respect of Comforter, Son, and Father.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is from John 3 and does not bear on v.7.

schools.summary: 'Reprove' = convict/convince by proof (elegxei; 3:20; 8:46); the Spirit carries on what Jesus began (7:7), first to Jews then Gentiles.
Calvin: Christ praises the gift by its effect — the Spirit will not only guide the church but subdue/convict the world.
Clarke/Wesley: a clear, doubt-removing conviction; the Spirit's twofold office — convicting the world, guiding believers (v.12).
Ellicott: not the sin of unbelief only but sin in general, with unbelief as its cause; Barnes: rejecting Christ is the chief sin.
Calvin: conviction begins with the demonstration of sin; Gill applies it to the Jews' rejection charged at Pentecost (Acts 2:36-37).
Clarke/Wesley: the sin of not receiving the accredited Messiah; unbelief 'the confluence of all sins.'

debates.question: Is the sin convicted unbelief itself, or sin in general rooted in unbelief?

sides.position: sin in general, with unbelief as its cause

schools.summary: 'Righteousness' = Christ's own vindicated innocence, proved by his going to the Father (resurrection/ascension); against the charge that he was an impostor.
Calvin: the steps in order — conviction of sin makes men hunger for the righteousness the Spirit reveals in the ascended Christ.
Wesley/Clarke: 'I could not go to the Father if I were not righteous' — his return is the proof of his holiness.

debates.question: Is the 'righteousness' Christ's personal innocence or his imputed/justifying righteousness?

themes: because I go to the Father

schools.summary: The prince of this world 'hath been judged' (perfect, stands condemned; 12:31; 14:30); the cross was the condemnation of Satan, proof God judges his foes.
Lightfoot/Calvin: Satan judged when Christ overcame him by death (Heb 2:14); the gospel manifests the world set in order by Christ's victory.
Clarke/Wesley: Satan's usurped power restrained and to be destroyed (Col 2:15), yet those who reject deliverance remain his slaves.
The progressive nature of revelation — the untaught cannot yet bear the full teaching (1 Cor 3:1; Heb 5:11-14); the Spirit will supply it (v.13).
Theophylact: heretics abuse this to license new fables, but it grants no such thing; Hilary fixes the Spirit's procession from the Father, sent by the Son.
Wesley: the 'many things' are given not in uncertain traditions but in Acts, the Epistles, and Revelation.
A full promise of apostolic inspiration — the Spirit (personal, 'ekeinos') guides them into the fulness of truth in founding the church.
Calvin: the Spirit is the perfect Master, given that the apostles might hand on the wisdom received; Cyril: Father gives to Son, Son shares with the Spirit.
Augustine: 'not of himself' shows the Spirit speaks from the Father and Son, the harmony of the Triune working.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is from John 3 and does not bear on v.13.

schools.summary: The Spirit's glory is to glorify Christ — a definite promise to interpret him to the apostles (fulfilled at Pentecost and in Paul).
Calvin: the Spirit erects no new kingdom but confirms Christ's glory — against the notion that the gospel is mere first lessons superseded by a higher school.
Cyril: the Father gives to the Son, the Son shares with the Spirit — the Triune harmony in revelation.
'All the Father hath are mine' — a claim no creature could make (Mt 28:18; 11:27); the Spirit takes 'of mine' because Son and Father share all.
Calvin: spoken 'in the person of the Mediator' — he received from the Father all he communicates by the Spirit, so the Father remains the fountain (John 1:16).
Wesley/Clarke: 'could any creature say this?' — a plain proof of Christ's equality with God.

themes: all the Father hath are mine

schools.summary: First 'little while' = till his death (7:33; 13:33), second = death to resurrection; 'ye shall see me' of the risen, spiritual seeing (16:22).
Chrysostom: after comforting he again 'depresses' them to accustom them to his absence; Augustine notes the saying's obscurity to them.
Calvin: he forewarns that they may bear the parting courageously and long more ardently for the Spirit, undesired while Christ was bodily present.
'Some of the disciples' confer among themselves, puzzled by the seeming contradiction and the 'going to the Father' (14:3); they talk to one another, not to Jesus.
Gill: their grief and pre-Pentecost ignorance leave them unable to grasp the saying — an illustration of v.12.

themes: the riddle of the little whiles

schools.summary: The disciples cease questioning Jesus and confess to one another, 'we know not what he saith' — baffled by the 'little while' of v.16.
Gill: the perplexity became universal, for the Spirit who would guide them (v.13) was not yet given.

themes: we cannot tell what he saith

schools.summary: Jesus perceives their unspoken desire to ask (2:25; 6:6) and answers it; the enigma had done its work of rousing inquiry.
Gill: a proof of his deity — he knows the secrets of the heart; Calvin: he cures their ignorance, and we must learn humbly, not in pride or sloth.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is from another passage and does not bear on v.19.

schools.summary: Violent contrast — the disciples weep at the cross while the world rejoices, but their sorrow turns to joy at the resurrection (Lk 23:27; John 20:20).
Calvin: he foretells the parting and promises a speedy return, showing the Spirit's aid is needed for the severe temptation of his death.
The travail-image, common in the prophets (Isa 26:17; 66:7-8; Jer 4:31), pictures sharp but purposeful pain forgotten in the joy of birth.
Calvin: the sorrow not only precedes the joy but contains its ground — the anguish itself brings forth the gladness.
After the resurrection their grief turns to a joy so grounded in his rising that no persecution can take it away — remarkably fulfilled.
Calvin: the joy's value lies in its perpetuity — unlike the world's fading joys, this is secure in the risen Christ.
Wesley: no warrant that believers must enter darkness — peace and the witness of sonship are lost only through sin, temptation, or disorder.
'Ask me nothing' = question no more (the Spirit illumines them), and/or request no more of me; but ask the Father in my name and he will give.
Chrysostom: 'in that day' (risen) they will not say 'show us the Father' (14:8); the resurrection-era of clear knowledge.
Calvin: a grace of the Spirit giving great light; Wesley: a 'charte blanche' — whatsoever ye ask the Father in my name, he will give.

debates.question: Does 'ye shall ask me nothing' mean question or request?

sides.position: question no more — they will be inwardly taught
both question and request, the access now to the Father

themes: ask the Father in my name

schools.summary: 'Hitherto' they had not prayed in Christ's name; now they are to — a plea for worship of Christ like the Father; the end is fulness of joy (v.22).
Calvin: the fathers never prayed without a Mediator; now that Mediator is openly Christ, in whose name they ask.
Clarke: ask in his name and receive the salvation requested, that your joy may be full.
Spoken 'in parables/proverbs' (10:6); the coming hour (resurrection-to-ascension, Acts 1:3, and Pentecost) brings plain showing of the Father.
Calvin: meant to give courage — the present obscurity is not because Christ would not be understood, but awaits fuller progress.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is from another passage and does not bear on v.25.

schools.summary: Not a denial of Christ's intercession (14:16; 17:9; Heb 7:25; 1 John 2:1) but a stress on the Father's own love for the disciples.
Calvin: the seeming contradiction resolved — the Father refuses nothing asked of his Son; no reluctant God needing to be won over.
Clarke/Wesley: it nowise implies he will not pray — the Father needs no entreaty, loving them for the faith he wrought in them.

themes: I say not I will pray

schools.summary: 'Loveth' (phileō, warm love) — God's friendly love for the disciples (cf. agapaō of the world, 3:16); grounded in their loving Christ and believing he came from God (14:21, 23).
Calvin: the only bond of union with God is union with Christ by a sincere, affectionate faith; Gill: the Father loved them of himself, from everlasting.
Clarke's fourfold creed — from the Father (eternity), into the world (incarnation), out of the world (death), to the Father (ascension): the whole gospel in brief.
The aorist 'came out' (the Incarnation) and perfect 'am come' (now permanent); heaven, not earth, is his home, to which he returns.
Calvin: it draws attention to the divine power in Christ — faith rests on his deity, without which death and resurrection would avail little.

themes: from the Father, to the Father

schools.summary: The emphatic 'now' — his plain word (v.28) has resolved their perplexity over v.16, and they declare themselves satisfied.
Chrysostom: refreshed at being in the Father's favour, they say they are sure he knows all things.
Calvin: a real but immature gladness — they did not yet fully understand, but 'the mere odour' of the consolation refreshed them.
Christ's reading of their unspoken thoughts (v.19) opened their minds; they confess his omniscience as proof he came from God.
Wesley: they echo back his words of v.27 — 'we believe in God; we believe also in thee.'
'Do ye now believe?' — their faith is genuine (17:8) but self-confident and untested; Christ puts them to self-examination.
Calvin: a gentle reproof of their security — we never know our distance from full faith until serious trial reveals it.

outliers.note: The JFB note merged here is from another passage and does not bear on v.31.

schools.summary: The hour has virtually begun (perfect; 17:1); they will be scattered each to his home (Mt 26:31), supposing his work defeated.
Calvin: forsaken by all, Christ loses nothing of his dignity — his glory founded on himself, for he is God; the Father is with him.

themes: yet not alone — the Father with me

schools.summary: Peace 'in me' (14:27), found nowhere else; 'be of good cheer' = courage in danger; 'I have overcome the world' (perfect of settled victory; 12:31).
Calvin: he shows how needful the consolations were — believers must expect many afflictions and so exercise patience.
Clarke: the peace of God includes all blessedness — light, strength, comfort, the sense of divine favour.
